responses.strategic_rationale: The project navigates the tension between delivering high-fidelity, longitudinal parasomnia data within a residential Bonn facility and the practical constraints of safety, privacy, staffing, and cost. The selected levers emphasize tiered sensing to balance data richness with participant comfort, robust data governance to enable responsible data sharing, targeted recruitment to reach adequate sample size without bias, rigorous adjudication to preserve scoring validity, productive external collaboration to accelerate validation and dissemination, and a flexible staffing model to maintain safety without inflating fixed costs. Together these levers aim to produce credible phenotyping and validated event-triage tools within budget, while preserving ethics and enabling scalable follow-on work pending Phase 2 funding. Risk includes potential data gaps, slower external validation, and coordination overhead.

levers.name: Tiered sensing cadence and escalation policy
levers.consequences: Tiered sensing cadence reduces participant burden and lowers nightly staff workload by relying on low-burden sensors most nights; however, escalation depends on predefined triggers and may miss infrequent events if thresholds are set too conservatively. A downstream implication is increased need for post-hoc review and potential data gaps that could affect time-to-detection analyses.
options: Implement nightly low-burden monitoring with dry EEG headbands, mattress sensors, and infrared cameras, escalating to enhanced PSG on confirmed signals.
Maintain a fixed schedule of enhanced PSG nights for all participants to maximize uniform data collection.
Establish rule-based escalation criteria that trigger enhanced nights when predefined signal combinations occur while preserving low-burden monitoring otherwise.
levers.review_lever: Tiered sensing cadence vs. continuous event capture. Weakness: Thresholds may miss sporadic events or introduce bias in detection.

levers.name: Data governance and privacy controls for sensitive recordings
levers.consequences: Strengthened data governance improves participant trust and regulatory compliance, enabling selective external research access. However, strict controls can slow data sharing, limit rapid external validation, and require additional administrative overhead.
options: Adopt de-identified physiological data with controlled access and formal data use agreements to enable external research while restricting raw video.
Limit public data sharing to aggregated metrics and de-identify all sensor streams, with video footage retained on secure, non-public storage.
Create a governance charter requiring dual data steward approvals for any external data request and periodic privacy impact reviews.
levers.review_lever: Data governance vs. access speed. Weakness: Strict controls can impede external validation and slow research progress.

levers.name: Recruitment and retention incentives and pathways
levers.consequences: Expanded recruitment channels should improve enrollment velocity and diversity, reducing timeline risk. However, broader outreach increases screening workload and may raise screening costs, plus potential selection bias if referrals differ by region.
options: Expand recruitment channels to include regional neurologists and patient advocacy groups with standardized pre-screening.
Implement a structured consent and onboarding program with staged participation options to reduce drop-offs.
Adopt a matched control recruitment framework with transparent eligibility criteria and equitable compensation terms.
levers.review_lever: Enrollment velocity vs. representativeness. Weakness: Expanded channels may increase screening burden and risk selection bias.

levers.name: Independent adjudication quality framework
levers.consequences: Dual scoring with calibration sustains high inter-rater reliability and reduces misclassification risk. The trade-off is higher labor cost and longer adjudication cycles that may limit throughput during peak periods.
options: Maintain dual independent scoring with scheduled calibration sessions to sustain inter-rater reliability.
Incorporate an external blinded adjudicator for a random sample of events to validate internal scoring.
Adopt a consensus-driven adjudication process with predefined tie-break rules and documentation of disagreements.
levers.review_lever: Consistency of scoring vs. throughput. Weakness: Dual scoring increases time to data availability.

levers.name: External collaboration and publication governance
levers.consequences: Structured collaborations accelerate validation and dissemination and can enhance credibility through replication. However, partner obligations and IP terms may constrain publication timing or data sharing.
options: Establish collaboration with a second sleep center for replication and cross-validation.
Develop a pre-registration and publication plan that requires open methods sections and data descriptors, with clear data sharing terms.
Create an industry partnership with pharma as a data access partner with predefined publication windows and rights.
levers.review_lever: Open collaboration vs. IP/publication control. Weakness: Partner terms may constrain publication timing or data sharing.

levers.name: Clinical workflow optimization and safety staffing
levers.consequences: Flexible staffing maintains safety and throughput while controlling costs during variable event load. The trade-off includes potential fatigue risk for on-call staff and higher coordination overhead.
options: Implement flexible staffing with on-call coverage to match anticipated event load and maintain safe ratios.
Standardize night shift rosters to ensure consistent coverage across all eight suites with clear handover protocols.
Introduce cross-training so technicians can perform multi-role tasks during low event periods.
levers.review_lever: Safety margin vs. cost containment. Weakness: Flexible staffing increases coordination complexity and fatigue risk.

responses.summary: Add an external audit of escalation thresholds to Tiered sensing cadence to ensure consistent event capture.

responses.strategic_rationale: This set of levers targets the Bonn residential parasomnia program's core tensions: enrollability and sample representativeness, fidelity and privacy of multi-stream sensing, participant burden versus retention, safety in a live clinical-like environment, data processing throughput, and disciplined scalability. By aligning recruitment workflows, privacy controls, compensation structures, on-site safety, semi-automated event triage, and staged governance, the plan seeks to sustain high-quality, longitudinal phenotyping while preserving ethical standards and operational flexibility. The levers collectively balance speed, cost, scope, and data integrity, enabling iterative learning and responsible expansion tied to verifiable milestones for Aim 1–3 outcomes.

levers.name: Pre-admission pathway optimization
levers.consequences: Enrollment efficiency improves with streamlined screening, shortening the path from referral to admission. Downstream, the approach may bias enrollment toward participants with easier travel and access to collateral histories, necessitating a plan to monitor sample representativeness. It may increase upfront screening resource use but reduce overall time to enrollment.
options: Streamline pre-admission screening with a dedicated participant navigator to improve enrollment yield.
Centralize collateral history collection and remote eligibility checks to reduce travel burden on participants.
Partner with regional sleep clinics to conduct initial assessments before referral to Bonn site.
levers.review_lever: Enrollment efficiency vs data completeness. Weakness: Streamlining screening may reduce the depth of collateral data and increase risk of misclassification.

levers.name: Video and audio data governance for naturalistic monitoring
levers.consequences: Stronger data governance reduces exposure risk for bedroom video and audio streams and supports regulatory compliance. Downstream, strict controls may limit certain analyses and slow data access for approved researchers. The design will require ongoing policy maintenance and clear justification for each data access request.
options: Enforce role-based access controls and encrypted storage across all ecological sensor streams.
Limit public sharing to de-identified datasets while restricting bedroom video to approved researchers.
Implement on-site privacy review checkpoints before introducing any new capture modality.
levers.review_lever: Privacy vs data utility. Weakness: Strong anonymization may limit downstream analyses and cross-study comparability.

levers.name: Participant compensation strategy and retention paths
levers.consequences: Participant compensation can improve retention during multi-week stays and reduce dropout versus logistical barriers. Downstream, compensation-based recruitment may bias toward participants more motivated by financial incentives, and the program must monitor this risk. Cost pressures could be balanced by milestone-based payouts.
options: Tie per-night compensation to attendance milestones and completion of required nights.
Provide lodging and travel subsidies to reduce dropout due to logistical barriers.
Offer flexible stay options and community support services to accommodate participant needs.
levers.review_lever: Enrollment incentives vs representativeness. Weakness: Compensation-driven recruitment may bias the sample toward financially motivated participants.

levers.name: Residential safety and risk mitigation
levers.consequences: Enhanced safety protocols reduce incident risk for nocturnal events and protect both participants and staff. Downstream, the added staffing and equipment costs increase operating expenses and may impact bed availability or scheduling flexibility. Regular drills also create process discipline that can improve data integrity through consistent procedures.
options: Implement heightened nocturnal monitoring and a rapid-response protocol for medical events.
Establish trained safety officers on site during all nights with staggered shifts.
Perform quarterly safety drills and annual facility safety audits to close gaps.
levers.review_lever: Safety coverage vs cost and staffing. Weakness: Increased training and safety staffing raises upfront costs and may reduce bed turnover if not managed.

levers.name: Event triage tool development and validation
levers.consequences: A semi-automated event triage can dramatically reduce manual scoring time and accelerate data processing. Downstream, there is a risk that rule-based triage misclassifies events requiring nuanced interpretation. Ongoing human validation remains essential to maintain reliability.
options: Develop a semi-automated event triage prototype using rule-based scoring and human-in-the-loop calibration.
Benchmark triage outputs against dual human scoring and publish methodology.
Run a blind annotation campaign to expand labeled events under strict privacy controls.
levers.review_lever: Automation vs accuracy. Weakness: Rule-based triage may miss nuanced events requiring expert judgement.

levers.name: Governance and scaling decision framework
levers.consequences: An explicit governance framework ties expansion to measurable milestones in enrollment and data yield. Downstream, rigid gates may slow timely scaling or hamper adaptation when new opportunities arise. The framework should include built-in review cycles and clear triggers for responsive adjustment.
options: Adopt staged go/no-go gates linked to enrollment and event capture metrics.
Define budgetary triggers for board review and resource reallocation.
Involve external peer review at interim milestones to validate methodology.
levers.review_lever: Expansion gates vs agility. Weakness: Gates may slow response to opportunities and risk stagnation.

responses.summary: Add 'tie per-night compensation to milestone completion' to Participant compensation strategy and retention paths.

responses.strategic_rationale: The Bonn residential parasomnia unit faces core tensions around delivering safe, continuous on-site observation within a domestic, non-clinical environment while maintaining rigorous data governance and enabling meaningful recruitment and longitudinal phenotyping. Trade-offs include safety staffing costs versus 24/7 coverage, privacy protections versus timely data sharing, and the need to enroll and retain participants against the burden of extended stays. Six levers focus on safety architecture, governance, recruitment efficiency, event-triage reliability, environment design, and governance of external partnerships to create a sustainable, rigorously annotated resource for longitudinal parasomnia research.

levers.name: Staffing and Safety Coverage Architecture
levers.consequences: Direct effect: ensures rapid safety response during nocturnal events and reduces incident duration. Downstream: increases personnel costs and scheduling complexity; potential for fatigue if shifts are not managed and event frequency is low.
options: Establish a dedicated on-site safety team with 24/7 coverage and overlapping shifts to guarantee immediate response to nocturnal events.
Adopt a hybrid model with on-site leads during high-risk windows and remote escalation during quieter periods to balance costs and safety.
Maintain a centralized monitoring desk that triggers on-site responders within minutes for flagged events while preserving resident autonomy.
levers.review_lever: Immediate safety coverage vs. staffing costs. Weakness: The options fail to account for long-term burnout risk and regulatory staffing constraints.

levers.name: Data Governance and External Access
levers.consequences: Direct effect: strengthens privacy and control over data flows. Downstream: could slow external collaboration due to access controls; trade-off: governance overhead adds administrative burden.
options: Create a formal data access committee that reviews every external data request and enforces de-identification standards before release.
Publish de-identified datasets to a controlled repository with tiered access levels and scheduled audits of usage.
Require reciprocal data-sharing agreements with collaborators and annual compliance reviews to maintain ongoing governance.
levers.review_lever: Privacy vs. collaboration. Weakness: The options may slow collaboration due to access controls.

levers.name: Recruitment and Retention Funnel
levers.consequences: Direct effect: improves enrollment efficiency and reduces dropout risk through pre-screening and enhanced support. Downstream: requires more upfront coordination with regional clinics and may increase administrative load.
options: Implement targeted pre-screening via regional clinics to enrich for eligible participants before admission.
Offer flexible stay scheduling and enhanced participant support to reduce dropout risk during extended monitoring.
Expand outreach through professional networks and patient societies to widen recruitment channels beyond hospital referrals.
levers.review_lever: Enrollment speed vs. participant burden. Weakness: The options may overcomplicate recruitment pipelines.

levers.name: Event-Triage and Scoring Protocols
levers.consequences: Direct effect: improves reliability of event identification and reduces misclassification. Downstream: increases reviewer workload due to adjudication demands; may slow throughput if not managed.
options: Enforce double independent scoring with adjudication for any discordant episodes to improve reliability.
Develop a standardized event taxonomy and scoring rubric to minimize inter-rater variability.
Implement a rule-based triage queue that prioritizes high-likelihood parasomnia events for rapid review by senior scorers.
levers.review_lever: Consistency of event scoring vs. review workload. Weakness: The options may increase reviewer fatigue due to adjudication demands.

levers.name: Naturalistic Environment and Observation Integrity
levers.consequences: Direct: preserves a domestic ambiance while enabling safe observation. Downstream: increases construction and maintenance costs and may constrain scalability if modular design is not easily adaptable.
options: Configure rooms as modular zones that preserve a domestic feel while enabling safe isolation during events.
Upgrade non-intrusive sensing layers (e.g., mattress sensors, infrared cameras) to reduce disturbance and maintain data fidelity.
Phase expansion to 12 suites only after data quality benchmarks are met and false alarm rates stabilize at an acceptable level.
levers.review_lever: Environmental realism vs. scalability. Weakness: The options may incur higher costs or limit expansion if scalability is challenging.

levers.name: External Collaboration and Governance Framework
levers.consequences: Direct: clarifies roles and decision rights, enabling steady progress. Downstream: may slow decisions if the external board becomes a bottleneck; potential misalignment with academic timelines.
options: Formalize an external scientific advisory board with clear milestones and defined decision rights.
Negotiate a phased collaboration protocol with regional sleep centers to share recruitment and validation tasks.
Establish a research-first collaboration policy with pharmaceutical partners, including a defined pre-publication review window to protect academic authorship.
levers.review_lever: Governance complexity vs. agility. Weakness: The options may slow decision-making due to board oversight.

responses.summary: Add biannual external data-access audit to Data Governance and External Access.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever` (one field, two sentences):
     First sentence names the core tension. Second sentence identifies a weakness.
     Example: "Controls centralization vs. local autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to account for transition costs."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.
   - For `summary`:
     One sentence prescribing a concrete addition to a specific lever.
     Example: "Add 'partner with a regional distributor for last-mile logistics' to Supply Chain Strategy."

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach — not a vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
